name: Tethra
description: Tethra is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, primarily associated with the Otherworld and the sea. He is often depicted as a king or ruler of Mag Mell, a paradisiacal realm beyond the mortal world, a land of eternal youth and beauty. Tethra's origins are somewhat obscure, but he is generally considered to be a Fomorian king, pre-dating the Tuatha Dé Danann's arrival in Ireland. He is described as a powerful and enigmatic figure, wielding considerable influence over the sea and its inhabitants. His association with the sea links him to concepts of the afterlife, as the sea was often seen as a gateway to the Otherworld. Tethra's role in mythology is multifaceted. While he is a king of the Otherworld, he is also connected to battles and conflict. He is mentioned in some texts as having lost an eye in battle, a common motif in Irish mythology signifying wisdom and sacrifice. Though a Fomorian, his interactions with the Tuatha Dé Danann are complex, sometimes adversarial and sometimes showing signs of cooperation or exchange. He is a figure of both power and mystery, embodying the allure and the danger of the Otherworld. Tethra's legacy persists in Irish folklore, where he is remembered as a ruler of a magical realm and a guardian of ancient secrets, representing the enduring power and mystery of the pre-Celtic inhabitants of Ireland.
subtitle: King of the Otherworld and the Sea

keyMyths.title: Tethra's Realm
keyMyths.description: Tethra rules over Mag Mell, a land of eternal youth and happiness. It is reached by crossing the sea and offers endless feasts and joys.

keyMyths.title: Loss of an Eye
keyMyths.description: Tethra lost an eye in battle, symbolizing a sacrifice for wisdom or power. This is a recurring motif in Irish mythology.

keyMyths.title: Relations with the Tuatha Dé Danann
keyMyths.description: Tethra's relationships with the Tuatha Dé Danann are complex, ranging from conflict to potential alliances. His role is ambiguous.
